stem:
A 16-day-old term baby girl is brought to the GP for her two-week check by her first-time mother. The mother mentions that her daughter's jaundice has not faded as expected and her nappies have looked unusually pale this week, almost like clay. She is exclusively breastfed and has regained her birth weight at day 14. Her mother is well, blood group O Rh-positive, and the cord blood from delivery was group A Rh-positive direct antiglobulin negative.

On examination the baby is alert, active and feeding well. She is visibly jaundiced to mid-thigh and her sclerae are deeply yellow. The liver edge is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin.

data_table.Examination: alert, active, jaundice to mid-thigh, sclerae deeply icteric, liver 3 cm below costal margin, spleen not palpable, pale stool seen in nappy
data_table.Investigations: total bilirubin 198 micromol/L, conjugated (direct) bilirubin 56 micromol/L (less than 17 or less than 20 percent of total), ALT 142 U/L, GGT 380 U/L, INR 1.1, FBC normal, urine reducing substances negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy with bilirubin reassessment in 6 hours and consider exchange
options.rationale: Phototherapy treats unconjugated bilirubin and is contraindicated for conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, where it can cause bronze baby syndrome without addressing the cholestatic cause. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Urgent paediatric hepatology referral for HIDA scintigraphy, hepatic ultrasound and TORCH screen
options.rationale: Conjugated bilirubin above 17 micromol/L or above 20 percent of total at any age is pathological cholestasis. Acholic stools and a firm liver point to biliary atresia, where Kasai portoenterostomy outcomes drop sharply if delayed past 60 days. Urgent referral, hepatobiliary imaging and cholangiogram are the AU pathway. (Source: RCH CPG: Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia)

options.text: Switch to a hypoallergenic formula and arrange community midwife review in 1 week
options.rationale: Cow's milk protein intolerance does not cause cholestatic jaundice with pale stools. Switching formula delays workup for biliary atresia. (Source: RCH CPG: Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia)

options.text: Reassure as breast milk jaundice and continue breastfeeding with repeat bilirubin in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Breast milk jaundice is unconjugated only. The presence of conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and acholic stools mandates urgent workup. Delay of 2 weeks could push past the 60-day window for optimal Kasai. (Source: RCH CPG: Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia)

options.text: Empirical phenobarbitone for 5 days then repeat liver function tests and reassess
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone has no role in the workup of neonatal cholestasis. It does not address biliary atresia and delays referral. (Source: RCH CPG: Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia)

explanation.summary: Any conjugated (direct) bilirubin above 17 micromol/L or above 20 percent of total bilirubin is pathological cholestasis and warrants urgent workup. Acholic stools, hepatomegaly and a deeply icteric infant past 14 days raise biliary atresia at the top of the differential. The window for successful Kasai portoenterostomy is narrow - native liver survival is markedly better when surgery is done before 60 days of age. The AU pathway is urgent paediatric hepatology referral for hepatic ultrasound, HIDA scintigraphy, TORCH screen, alpha-1 antitrypsin level and intraoperative cholangiogram.
key_points: Direct bilirubin above 17 micromol/L or 20% of total = cholestasis = urgent
Biliary atresia Kasai window: best outcomes before 60 days
Pale stools plus dark urine plus prolonged jaundice = red flag triad
Phototherapy contraindicated in conjugated jaundice
explanation.pearls: The 'stool colour card' programme used in some AU jurisdictions catches biliary atresia earlier than family report alone - if your hospital does not use one, asking parents to photograph nappies is a low-tech equivalent.

stem:
A 9-day-old term boy is brought to the ED by his parents because he has been increasingly difficult to feed, sleepy and floppy since yesterday. There has been no fever, no diarrhoea and no vomiting initially, although he vomited twice this morning. He was born at 39 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery weighing 3.4 kg and has lost 14 percent of his birth weight by today. His newborn bloodspot screen was taken on day 3 but the result is not yet available. He has no siblings.

On arrival he is hypotonic, peripherally mottled with capillary refill of 4 seconds and centrally cool. His scrotum is normally formed with descended testes. There is no rash and no fontanelle bulge.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 188 /min, blood pressure 52/28 mmHg, respiratory rate 62 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.2 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: hypotonic, mottled peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds, normally formed male genitalia with descended testes, fontanelle sunken
data_table.Investigations: sodium 122 mmol/L (135 to 145), potassium 7.4 mmol/L (3.5 to 5.5), glucose 2.1 mmol/L (3.5 to 6.0), urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L, bedside venous blood gas pH 7.22, bicarbonate 14 mmol/L, lactate 4.6 mmol/L, ECG peaked T waves in V2 to V5 without QRS widening

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous 10 percent calcium gluconate 0.5 mL/kg with nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as priority intervention for hyperkalaemia
options.rationale: Calcium gluconate stabilises the myocardium but does not address the underlying mineralocorticoid and glucocorticoid deficiency. Salbutamol shifts potassium intracellularly but is adjunctive. Without hydrocortisone this child will deteriorate. (Source: APEG paediatric endocrine emergencies)

options.text: Intravenous insulin 0.1 unit/kg with dextrose infusion and start oral fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily
options.rationale: Oral fludrocortisone has slow onset and cannot be relied on in shock. Insulin-dextrose addresses potassium but not the cortisol deficiency which is the precipitating crisis. (Source: APEG paediatric endocrine emergencies)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 25 mg with intravenous 0.9 percent saline 20 mL/kg bolus and 10 percent dextrose 2 mL/kg, then PICU admission
options.rationale: Salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) classically presents around days 7 to 14 with hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia and shock. IV hydrocortisone 25 mg in a neonate is the lifesaving dose, given immediately after a paired cortisol and 17-OHP sample. Fluid resuscitation with normal saline plus dextrose addresses shock and hypoglycaemia. PICU admission is appropriate. (Source: APEG paediatric endocrine emergencies)

options.text: Intravenous dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, normal saline 10 mL/kg and arrange MRI brain to exclude hypopituitarism
options.rationale: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid activity, so it does not address the salt-wasting component. It also interferes with paired cortisol sampling. Imaging is not the priority during shock. (Source: APEG paediatric endocrine emergencies)

options.text: Empirical intravenous cefotaxime, full septic workup including lumbar puncture, and resuscitate with 20 mL/kg 0.9 percent sodium chloride
options.rationale: While sepsis is on the differential, the biochemistry (Na 122, K 7.4, glucose 2.1) is classic for salt-wasting CAH and demands hydrocortisone, dextrose and salt urgently. Antibiotics can be added but cannot be the only intervention. LP should be deferred until stabilised. (Source: APEG paediatric endocrine emergencies)

explanation.summary: Salt-wasting 21-hydroxylase CAH presents in the second week of life with shock, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia and hypoglycaemia because aldosterone and cortisol cannot be made. Male infants are not detected by ambiguous genitalia at birth and may present in extremis before bloodspot results return. Lifesaving steps are IV hydrocortisone 25 mg in the neonate, normal saline plus 10 percent dextrose to address shock and hypoglycaemia, and paired cortisol and 17-OHP before steroid administration. Fludrocortisone is started later, orally, once stable.
key_points: Salt-wasting CAH classically presents day 7 to 14 of life
Biochemistry: Na low, K high, glucose low, acidosis
Hydrocortisone 25 mg IV in the neonate is lifesaving
Take paired cortisol and 17-OHP before steroids if not delaying
Fludrocortisone is oral and added once stable
explanation.pearls: Boys with classic salt-wasting CAH typically present after the bloodspot is taken but before its result is returned - the diagnosis is made clinically and confirmed retrospectively when 17-OHP is markedly elevated.

subtopic_detail: Refractory paediatric status epilepticus - third-line escalation
stem:
A 12-year-old girl with focal epilepsy on regular sodium valproate is brought in by ambulance during a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that has now been continuous for 28 minutes. Paramedics gave intramuscular midazolam 10 mg at 5 minutes and intranasal midazolam 5 mg at 12 minutes without effect. On ED arrival you give intravenous levetiracetam 60 mg/kg over 5 minutes, which finishes at minute 25. She continues to seize at minute 28 with rhythmic clonic activity of the right arm and face. Bedside glucose is 5.8 mmol/L and there are no toxic ingestion concerns. She has good IV access and is being bag-mask ventilated with oxygen.

The PICU registrar is on the phone and asks what you plan to do next.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 168 /min, blood pressure 132/82 mmHg, respiratory rate via bag-mask ventilation, SpO2 96% on FiO2 1.0 via bag-mask, temperature 37.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: ongoing right-sided focal motor activity with secondary generalisation, GCS 3, pupils 4 mm reactive bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: bedside glucose 5.8 mmol/L, venous blood gas pH 7.18 bicarbonate 16 mmol/L lactate 8.4 mmol/L, sodium 138 potassium 4.4 calcium ionised 1.18 mmol/L, paracetamol level negative, valproate level pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat intravenous levetiracetam 30 mg/kg over 5 minutes and reassess in 10 minutes
options.rationale: ESETT showed no benefit from repeat dosing of the same second-line agent and the AES/RCH algorithm moves to a different agent or to general anaesthesia at this point. Repeat dosing risks toxicity without benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Intravenous phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes as the next second-line agent
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is an alternative second-line agent but at minute 28 with two failed agents already, the AU paediatric algorithm moves to induction of general anaesthesia rather than serial second-line trials. Phenobarbitone also risks apnoea before intubation is established. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 10 minutes before further antiseizure medication
options.rationale: IV magnesium is reserved for eclampsia and known channelopathies with magnesium responsiveness. It is not an algorithm step in generalised tonic-clonic status epilepticus in a 12-year-old on valproate. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Rapid sequence induction with thiopentone 4 mg/kg or propofol 2 mg/kg with intubation and PICU transfer
options.rationale: She is in refractory status epilepticus (seizing despite a benzodiazepine and a non-benzodiazepine second-line agent at 25 plus minutes). RCH and AES escalate at this point to induction of general anaesthesia with thiopentone or propofol, intubation and continuous EEG monitoring in PICU to confirm seizure suppression. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Intravenous lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg as a third benzodiazepine dose and continue bag-mask ventilation
options.rationale: A third benzodiazepine dose markedly increases respiratory depression without changing seizure outcome and is not in the AU paediatric algorithm. The patient has already had two midazolam doses. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

explanation.summary: Refractory status epilepticus is seizure activity continuing despite adequate doses of a benzodiazepine followed by a non-benzodiazepine second-line agent. The AU paediatric algorithm at this point moves to rapid sequence induction with thiopentone or propofol, intubation, and transfer to PICU for continuous EEG-guided burst suppression. Serial second-line agents do not improve outcomes (ESETT) and delay definitive control. Bedside glucose, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium and toxic ingestion screens are checked along the algorithm path.
key_points: Refractory SE = seizing despite benzo plus non-benzo second-line
Move to RSI with thiopentone or propofol, intubate, PICU
Serial second-line agents do not improve outcomes (ESETT)
Continuous EEG to confirm electrographic seizure suppression
Always check glucose, Na, Ca, Mg, toxicology along the algorithm
explanation.pearls: Even when motor activity stops after RSI, up to 40 percent of these patients remain in non-convulsive status on EEG - so EEG monitoring is part of the treatment, not just a workup tool.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum haemorrhage - mechanical step after failed pharmacological
stem:
A 32-year-old G3P2 has a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39 weeks of a 4.1 kg infant after a 12-hour labour augmented with oxytocin. Active management of the third stage was performed. By 30 minutes postpartum her estimated blood loss is 1800 mL with ongoing brisk haemorrhage and a soft, atonic uterus on bimanual palpation. The placenta is complete and there are no perineal or vaginal lacerations on inspection.

She has already received IV oxytocin 10 units then a 40-unit infusion in 1 litre Hartmann solution, IM ergometrine 250 micrograms, and IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes. She remains pale and tachycardic with ongoing PV bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 124 /min, blood pressure 92/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: uterus soft and poorly contracted at the umbilicus, ongoing brisk fresh PV loss, placenta intact on inspection, no genital tract laceration
data_table.Investigations: Hb 86 g/L (115 to 165), platelets 142 x10^9/L, fibrinogen 2.4 g/L, INR 1.1, crossmatched packed red cells available, group and antibody screen on file

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes and add a further 5 units of intramuscular ergometrine
options.rationale: A second dose of tranexamic acid is given at 30 minutes if bleeding continues, but ergometrine has a maximum total dose of 500 micrograms and is contraindicated by hypertension. The bigger issue is that pharmacology has failed and a mechanical step is needed. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Take to theatre for emergency peripartum hysterectomy under general anaesthesia and post-op ICU
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is a definitive step but follows balloon tamponade and uterine compression sutures. Going directly to hysterectomy without trying balloon tamponade misses an effective intermediate step. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Insert a Foley urinary catheter, continue bimanual compression and observe for response over 30 minutes
options.rationale: An indwelling catheter is part of resus but bimanual compression alone is a holding measure. After failure of multiple uterotonics with ongoing brisk haemorrhage, the next active step is dedicated intrauterine tamponade with theatre activated. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Administer rectal misoprostol 800 micrograms and reassess uterine tone in 15 to 30 minutes
options.rationale: Misoprostol is part of the uterotonic ladder but adds little once oxytocin, ergometrine and tranexamic acid have failed. The algorithm at this stage moves to mechanical tamponade. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Insert intrauterine balloon tamponade, activate theatre and massive transfusion protocol, call senior obstetrician
options.rationale: RANZCOG escalates from uterotonics plus tranexamic acid to mechanical tamponade when pharmacological measures fail. Intrauterine balloon tamponade resolves bleeding in around 80 percent of cases, buying time to activate theatre, blood products and a senior team. Simultaneous escalation is the AU standard. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Postpartum haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: The RANZCOG PPH algorithm progresses through uterotonics (oxytocin, ergometrine, carboprost or misoprostol), tranexamic acid, then mechanical measures (uterine balloon tamponade), then surgical measures (B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, internal iliac ligation), and finally hysterectomy. When pharmacological measures fail, intrauterine balloon tamponade with simultaneous theatre and massive transfusion protocol activation is the next active step. Balloon tamponade controls bleeding in around 80 percent of cases.
key_points: RANZCOG PPH ladder: uterotonics, TXA, balloon, sutures, vessel ligation, hysterectomy
Intrauterine balloon controls bleeding in around 80 percent
Ergometrine maximum total dose 500 micrograms, contraindicated by HTN
TXA second dose at 30 minutes if bleeding continues
Always activate MTP, theatre and senior team simultaneously
explanation.pearls: A condom-catheter tamponade is a documented effective improvisation when a Bakri is not immediately available - inflate to 250 to 500 mL with warm saline and tie around the catheter.

stem:
A 30-year-old G2P1 presents to delivery suite at 39 weeks in spontaneous labour with regular contractions every 4 minutes. Her cervix is 4 cm dilated and membranes are intact. A vaginal-rectal swab at 36 weeks was positive for Group B Streptococcus. She has no drug allergies and her previous baby was healthy with no neonatal sepsis. Antenatal care has been otherwise uncomplicated.

The midwife asks for your antibiotic plan before further progress.

data_table.Vital signs: vital signs within normal limits, foetal heart rate 138 /min with normal variability
data_table.Examination: cervix 4 cm dilated, 80 percent effaced, vertex at minus 1 station, membranes intact, foetal heart rate normal on CTG
data_table.Investigations: antenatal swab 36 weeks positive for Group B Streptococcus; no past adverse drug reactions documented

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the AU CDNA pathway recommend benzylpenicillin as the agent of choice for GBS intrapartum prophylaxis in penicillin-tolerant women: 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery, with at least 4 hours before birth giving optimal neonatal protection. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg every 8 hours during labour until delivery is completed
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve adequate amniotic fluid levels rapidly enough for intrapartum prophylaxis. IV is mandatory. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours until delivery
options.rationale: Cefazolin is the alternative for women with low-risk penicillin allergy (rash, no anaphylaxis). With no documented penicillin allergy, benzylpenicillin is preferred. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours until delivery without susceptibility check
options.rationale: Clindamycin is reserved for women with high-risk penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis) where the GBS isolate is clindamycin-susceptible. It is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 20 mg/kg every 8 hours until delivery is achieved
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with clindamycin-resistant GBS. It is third-line and not appropriate here. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

explanation.summary: GBS intrapartum prophylaxis is indicated for any woman with a positive antenatal screen, GBS bacteriuria during pregnancy, a previous infant with invasive GBS disease, or unknown GBS status with risk factors (less than 37 weeks, ruptured membranes 18 hours, intrapartum fever 38 degrees C). Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours is the agent of choice, with cefazolin for low-risk and clindamycin for high-risk penicillin allergy where susceptibility is confirmed. Optimal neonatal protection requires at least 4 hours of IV antibiotics before delivery.
key_points: GBS screening: vaginal-rectal swab at 35 to 37 weeks
First-line: benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load then 1.8 g q4h until delivery
Low-risk penicillin allergy: cefazolin
High-risk allergy (anaphylaxis): clindamycin if susceptible, otherwise vancomycin
Aim for 4 plus hours of antibiotic before birth
explanation.pearls: The benzylpenicillin loading dose is 3 g not 2.4 g in current RANZCOG - if you trained on older protocols, double-check the dose in your local maternity handbook.

stem:
A 28-year-old G2P0 with one previous first-trimester miscarriage presents to the early pregnancy assessment unit with 4 days of intermittent left iliac fossa pain and light brown spotting. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago. She is haemodynamically stable and pain is now mild without analgesia. She has no significant past medical history, is not on regular medications, and has reliable transport for follow-up.

Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus and a 22 mm left adnexal mass adjacent to but separate from the ovary, with no foetal pole and no visible cardiac activity. A small amount of free fluid is in the pouch of Douglas but no haemoperitoneum. Serum beta-hCG taken in clinic is 3200 IU/L. Renal function and liver function are normal.

data_table.Examination: abdomen soft, mild left iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism, speculum minimal brown discharge from cervix
data_table.Investigations: TVUS empty uterus, 22 mm left tubal adnexal mass, no foetal pole, no cardiac activity, small POD free fluid; serum beta-hCG 3200 IU/L; FBC normal; LFTs and creatinine normal; hepatitis serology negative; group O Rh-positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management option to offer first?

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG every 48 hours until below 5 IU/L
options.rationale: Expectant management is reserved for asymptomatic women with falling beta-hCG below 1500 IU/L. This patient has rising symptoms and a beta-hCG above the threshold. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m^2 with follow-up beta-hCG on day 4 and day 7
options.rationale: She meets RANZCOG criteria for methotrexate: unruptured tubal ectopic, mass less than 35 mm, no foetal cardiac activity, beta-hCG less than 5000 IU/L, no significant pain, normal renal and liver function, no contraindications, and reliable follow-up. Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m^2 is the first-line offer. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Surgery is indicated when medical management is contraindicated, has failed, or the patient prefers surgery after counselling. With criteria met for methotrexate, surgery is not the first offer. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingostomy with preservation of the affected fallopian tube structure
options.rationale: Salpingostomy is preferred over salpingectomy only when the contralateral tube is damaged or absent. It is otherwise not the first-line approach. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Oral mifepristone 200 mg followed by intravaginal misoprostol 800 micrograms in 48 hours
options.rationale: Mifepristone and misoprostol are used for intrauterine pregnancy termination. They are not effective for ectopic pregnancy and miss the diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG GPS: Ectopic pregnancy)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG criteria for offering single-dose methotrexate in tubal ectopic pregnancy: haemodynamically stable, unruptured, adnexal mass less than 35 mm, no foetal cardiac activity, beta-hCG less than 5000 IU/L, no significant pain, normal renal and liver function, no contraindications to methotrexate (active peptic ulcer, breastfeeding, immunodeficiency), and reliable follow-up. Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m^2 with day-4 and day-7 beta-hCG (expecting at least a 15 percent fall between these two days) succeeds in around 85 percent of selected cases. Surgical management is indicated when these criteria are not met or after failure of medical treatment.
key_points: RANZCOG methotrexate criteria: beta-hCG <5000, mass <35 mm, no FCA, no severe pain, reliable follow-up, no contraindications
Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m^2
Day-4 and day-7 beta-hCG check for 15% fall
Anti-D 250 IU IM in Rh-negative women
Surgical salpingectomy is preferred over salpingostomy when contralateral tube healthy
explanation.pearls: Methotrexate failure rate rises sharply above beta-hCG 5000 IU/L - even meeting other criteria, RANZCOG sets the threshold at 5000, not the older 3000 IU/L.
